title: Iron Deficiency Anaemia
oneLiner: Commonest anaemia globally; caused by blood loss or malabsorption; treat cause + replace iron — never assume diet alone.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute upper GI bleed** (e.g. varices, peptic ulcer) – look for haematemesis, melaena, hypotension, tachycardia, unstable Hb with low MCV – requires immediate resuscitation + endoscopy
- **Severe anaemia causing high-output cardiac failure** – look for resting tachycardia, raised JVP, fine crackles at lung bases, peripheral oedema – Hb < 60 g/L with haemodynamic instability needs urgent cross-match + discussion with cardiology/ITU
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fatigue, weakness, dyspnoea on exertion (insidious onset)
  - Pallor, cold intolerance, brittle nails
  - Pica (e.g. pagophagia – craving ice)
  - Restless legs syndrome
  - Angina (if ischaemic heart disease) or palpitations
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Conjunctival and palmar pallor
  - Koilonychia (spoon nails)
  - Glossitis (smooth, shiny tongue)
  - Angular cheilitis
  - Tachycardia, flow murmur (if severe)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Iron deficiency anaemia** (most likely) → low ferritin, low MCV, low iron, normal/negative genetic tests
- **Anaemia of chronic disease** → normal/high ferritin, low TIBC, normal transferrin saturation, coexistent inflammatory condition (e.g. RA, CKD)
- **Thalassaemia trait** → disproportionately low MCV for Hb; normal/raised ferritin; positive Hb electrophoresis; family history
- **Sideroblastic anaemia** → dimorphic red cell population, raised ferritin, ring sideroblasts on bone marrow
- **Lead toxicity** → basophilic stippling, neurological symptoms, occupational history
- **Splitter:** **Ferritin < 15 µg/L** (or < 30 in CKD) is diagnostic of IDA and differentiates from other microcytic anaemias (except in inflammatory states where ferritin can be falsely normal/high – check CRP with ferritin)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Check stool for overt/occult blood (FIT if > 60 years or high risk)
  - Pulse, BP, orthostatics if haemodynamically unstable
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, MCV, MCH, RDW, platelets – reactive thrombocytosis common)
  - Iron studies (ferritin, serum iron, TIBC/transferrin, transferrin saturation)
  - Ferritin **mandatory**: < 15 µg/L = definite IDA; check CRP concurrently (ferritin is acute phase reactant)
  - CRP + renal function + LFT (liver disease can affect iron metabolism)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Upper/lower GI endoscopy** if any positive FIT, visible bleeding, or unexplained IDA (all men + non-menstruating women need bidirectional endoscopy unless clear source)
  - CT colonography if colonoscopy inaccessible
  - **Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) + colonoscopy** is gold standard
  - Consider abdominal US if suspect GI malignancy or splenomegaly
- **Special tests:**
  - Coeliac serology (tTG IgA) + total IgA – for malabsorption (especially if no GI bleeding)
  - Serum B12, folate (can coexist)
  - Hb electrophoresis if thalassaemia suspected
  - Stool culture + ova/cysts if GI infection or malabsorption
  - Urinalysis for haematuria (urological source)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - NICE NG24 (2019): Adults with Hb < 110 g/L (pregnant) or < 120 g/L (non-pregnant) + MCV < 80 fL + ferritin < 15 µg/L
  - Transferrin saturation < 16% supports IDA (less specific)
- **Never-miss test:** **Faecal immunochemical test (FIT)** in any adult > 60 with unexplained IDA – if positive, proceed to GI endoscopy even if no visible bleeding
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (tachycardia, hypotension, active bleeding):
    - IV access × 2, cross-match 2–4 units
    - Transfuse one unit if Hb < 70 g/L (or < 80 g/L with cardiac comorbidity) – use NICE transfusion threshold (NG24)
    - IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg over 15 min if available) or ferric derisomaltose
  - If cause suspected acute GI bleed: urgent gastroenterology referral + PPI (e.g. omeprazole 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/h infusion)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Start oral iron immediately (if not contraindicated)
  - Arrange bidirectional endoscopy (OGD + colonoscopy) within 3–5 days for unexplained IDA
  - If coeliac serology positive → start gluten-free diet + refer gastroenterology
  - Document menstrual history ± gynaecology referral if menorrhagia suspected
  - Check response: reticulocyte count on day 7, Hb rise by 2 weeks (aim 8–10 g/L per week)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue oral iron for 3–6 months after Hb normalised to replenish stores
  - If oral iron fails (intolerance, malabsorption, continued bleeding): switch to IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or ferric derisomaltose per local protocol)
  - Address underlying cause: treat menorrhagia (e.g. tranexamic acid, Mirena IUS), treat coeliac, refer for surgery if GI malignancy
  - Clinical follow-up in 4–6 weeks to confirm Hb normalisation and ensure cause been addressed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann's if NBM or hypovolaemic; rate per haemodynamic status (no routine fluid for stable)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low-molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) – unless active bleeding risk
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if planning endoscopy within 6–8 h; otherwise normal diet (iron-rich foods)
  - **Observation frequency:** 4‑hourly observations if stable; if Hb < 70 or active bleed: 30–60 min initially until stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent:**
  - Ferrous sulphate 200 mg (65 mg elemental iron) **PO TDS** (starting dose; reduce to OD/BD if intolerance)
  - Duration: until Hb normal + 3 months for store replenishment
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ferrous fumarate 210 mg (68 mg elemental iron) PO BD
  - Ferrous gluconate 300 mg (35 mg elemental iron) PO TDS
  - Oral iron suspension (e.g. ferrous sulfate solution 125 mg/5 mL) for dysphagia
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid oral iron with antacids, tetracyclines, bisphosphonates, fluoroquinolones (altered absorption – separate by 2–4 h)
  - Caution in inflammatory bowel disease (may exacerbate flares)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment for oral iron in renal/hepatic impairment
  - IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose): use with caution in severe hepatic impairment (check local policy)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Iron overdose (accidental ingestion in children)** – do not exceed prescribed dose; acute iron toxicity causes severe gastritis, metabolic acidosis, hepatic failure. Never prescribe multiple courses simultaneously.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - GI side effects (nausea, constipation, diarrhoea, black stools – warn patient)
  - Iron overdose (accidental) – paediatric risk
  - Anaphylaxis to IV iron (rare; ensure resuscitation equipment available)
- **Chronic:**
  - Iron overload (rare in oral iron unless haemochromatosis coexists) – monitor ferritin if prolonged high-dose
  - Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH) – check LDH + haptoglobin if unexplained iron deficiency with haemolysis
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent if underlying cause treatable and iron stores repleted; Hb usually normalises within 8–12 weeks with adherence
- Poor prognosis if cause is advanced malignancy (colon cancer, gastric cancer) – requires urgent triple-referral (2-week-wait for endoscopy)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check ferritin with a CRP** – if CRP elevated and ferritin normal, you cannot rule out IDA (ferritin is acute-phase reactant); consider transferrin saturation < 16% as alternative.
- **In women of childbearing age with IDA, always ask about menorrhagia** and consider gynaecology referral plus IUS if desired.
- **If oral iron fails after 4 weeks (Hb rise < 10 g/L), re-assess compliance, then check for ongoing bleeding, coeliac disease, or need for IV iron.**
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not transfuse stable IDA with Hb > 70 g/L without significant symptoms** – unnecessary transfusion risk (allergic reactions, TRALI, iron overload) + does not treat cause.
- **Do not skip endoscopy in any adult > 50 with unexplained IDA** even if FIT negative – small bowel lesion (angiodysplasia, tumour, Crohn’s) may be missed.
- **Do not stop iron once Hb normalises** – iron stores take 3–6 months to replenish; early cessation leads to recurrence.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening dyspnoea, palpitations, black/tarry stools, haematemesis, new abdominal pain
- **Follow-up:** GP repeat FBC + ferritin in 6–8 weeks; if Hb not improved by > 10 g/L, arrange prompt clinic review
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / on‑call medical registrar if:**
  - Hb < 60 g/L with symptoms (chest pain, dyspnoea, syncope)
  - Signs of active GI bleeding (haematemesis, melaena) – need OGD within 24 h
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially tachycardia + hypotension)
- **Criteria for ICU:**
  - Haemodynamic instability despite 2 units PRBC + fluid
  - Suspected acute GI bleed with persistent shock

rawMarkdown:
## Iron Deficiency Anaemia
**One-liner:** Commonest anaemia globally; caused by blood loss or malabsorption; treat cause + replace iron — never assume diet alone.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute upper GI bleed** (e.g. varices, peptic ulcer) – look for haematemesis, melaena, hypotension, tachycardia, unstable Hb with low MCV – requires immediate resuscitation + endoscopy
- **Severe anaemia causing high-output cardiac failure** – look for resting tachycardia, raised JVP, fine crackles at lung bases, peripheral oedema – Hb < 60 g/L with haemodynamic instability needs urgent cross-match + discussion with cardiology/ITU

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fatigue, weakness, dyspnoea on exertion (insidious onset)
  - Pallor, cold intolerance, brittle nails
  - Pica (e.g. pagophagia – craving ice)
  - Restless legs syndrome
  - Angina (if ischaemic heart disease) or palpitations
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Conjunctival and palmar pallor
  - Koilonychia (spoon nails)
  - Glossitis (smooth, shiny tongue)
  - Angular cheilitis
  - Tachycardia, flow murmur (if severe)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Iron deficiency anaemia** (most likely) → low ferritin, low MCV, low iron, normal/negative genetic tests
- **Anaemia of chronic disease** → normal/high ferritin, low TIBC, normal transferrin saturation, coexistent inflammatory condition (e.g. RA, CKD)
- **Thalassaemia trait** → disproportionately low MCV for Hb; normal/raised ferritin; positive Hb electrophoresis; family history
- **Sideroblastic anaemia** → dimorphic red cell population, raised ferritin, ring sideroblasts on bone marrow
- **Lead toxicity** → basophilic stippling, neurological symptoms, occupational history
- **Splitter:** **Ferritin < 15 µg/L** (or < 30 in CKD) is diagnostic of IDA and differentiates from other microcytic anaemias (except in inflammatory states where ferritin can be falsely normal/high – check CRP with ferritin)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Check stool for overt/occult blood (FIT if > 60 years or high risk)
  - Pulse, BP, orthostatics if haemodynamically unstable
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, MCV, MCH, RDW, platelets – reactive thrombocytosis common)
  - Iron studies (ferritin, serum iron, TIBC/transferrin, transferrin saturation)
  - Ferritin **mandatory**: < 15 µg/L = definite IDA; check CRP concurrently (ferritin is acute phase reactant)
  - CRP + renal function + LFT (liver disease can affect iron metabolism)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Upper/lower GI endoscopy** if any positive FIT, visible bleeding, or unexplained IDA (all men + non-menstruating women need bidirectional endoscopy unless clear source)
  - CT colonography if colonoscopy inaccessible
  - **Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) + colonoscopy** is gold standard
  - Consider abdominal US if suspect GI malignancy or splenomegaly
- **Special tests:**
  - Coeliac serology (tTG IgA) + total IgA – for malabsorption (especially if no GI bleeding)
  - Serum B12, folate (can coexist)
  - Hb electrophoresis if thalassaemia suspected
  - Stool culture + ova/cysts if GI infection or malabsorption
  - Urinalysis for haematuria (urological source)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - NICE NG24 (2019): Adults with Hb < 110 g/L (pregnant) or < 120 g/L (non-pregnant) + MCV < 80 fL + ferritin < 15 µg/L
  - Transferrin saturation < 16% supports IDA (less specific)
- **Never-miss test:** **Faecal immunochemical test (FIT)** in any adult > 60 with unexplained IDA – if positive, proceed to GI endoscopy even if no visible bleeding

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (tachycardia, hypotension, active bleeding):
    - IV access × 2, cross-match 2–4 units
    - Transfuse one unit if Hb < 70 g/L (or < 80 g/L with cardiac comorbidity) – use NICE transfusion threshold (NG24)
    - IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg over 15 min if available) or ferric derisomaltose
  - If cause suspected acute GI bleed: urgent gastroenterology referral + PPI (e.g. omeprazole 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/h infusion)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Start oral iron immediately (if not contraindicated)
  - Arrange bidirectional endoscopy (OGD + colonoscopy) within 3–5 days for unexplained IDA
  - If coeliac serology positive → start gluten-free diet + refer gastroenterology
  - Document menstrual history ± gynaecology referral if menorrhagia suspected
  - Check response: reticulocyte count on day 7, Hb rise by 2 weeks (aim 8–10 g/L per week)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue oral iron for 3–6 months after Hb normalised to replenish stores
  - If oral iron fails (intolerance, malabsorption, continued bleeding): switch to IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or ferric derisomaltose per local protocol)
  - Address underlying cause: treat menorrhagia (e.g. tranexamic acid, Mirena IUS), treat coeliac, refer for surgery if GI malignancy
  - Clinical follow-up in 4–6 weeks to confirm Hb normalisation and ensure cause been addressed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann's if NBM or hypovolaemic; rate per haemodynamic status (no routine fluid for stable)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low-molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) – unless active bleeding risk
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if planning endoscopy within 6–8 h; otherwise normal diet (iron-rich foods)
  - **Observation frequency:** 4‑hourly observations if stable; if Hb < 70 or active bleed: 30–60 min initially until stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent:**
  - Ferrous sulphate 200 mg (65 mg elemental iron) **PO TDS** (starting dose; reduce to OD/BD if intolerance)
  - Duration: until Hb normal + 3 months for store replenishment
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ferrous fumarate 210 mg (68 mg elemental iron) PO BD
  - Ferrous gluconate 300 mg (35 mg elemental iron) PO TDS
  - Oral iron suspension (e.g. ferrous sulfate solution 125 mg/5 mL) for dysphagia
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid oral iron with antacids, tetracyclines, bisphosphonates, fluoroquinolones (altered absorption – separate by 2–4 h)
  - Caution in inflammatory bowel disease (may exacerbate flares)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment for oral iron in renal/hepatic impairment
  - IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose): use with caution in severe hepatic impairment (check local policy)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Iron overdose (accidental ingestion in children)** – do not exceed prescribed dose; acute iron toxicity causes severe gastritis, metabolic acidosis, hepatic failure. Never prescribe multiple courses simultaneously.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - GI side effects (nausea, constipation, diarrhoea, black stools – warn patient)
  - Iron overdose (accidental) – paediatric risk
  - Anaphylaxis to IV iron (rare; ensure resuscitation equipment available)
- **Chronic:**
  - Iron overload (rare in oral iron unless haemochromatosis coexists) – monitor ferritin if prolonged high-dose
  - Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH) – check LDH + haptoglobin if unexplained iron deficiency with haemolysis

### Prognosis
- Excellent if underlying cause treatable and iron stores repleted; Hb usually normalises within 8–12 weeks with adherence
- Poor prognosis if cause is advanced malignancy (colon cancer, gastric cancer) – requires urgent triple-referral (2-week-wait for endoscopy)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check ferritin with a CRP** – if CRP elevated and ferritin normal, you cannot rule out IDA (ferritin is acute-phase reactant); consider transferrin saturation < 16% as alternative.
- **In women of childbearing age with IDA, always ask about menorrhagia** and consider gynaecology referral plus IUS if desired.
- **If oral iron fails after 4 weeks (Hb rise < 10 g/L), re-assess compliance, then check for ongoing bleeding, coeliac disease, or need for IV iron.**

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not transfuse stable IDA with Hb > 70 g/L without significant symptoms** – unnecessary transfusion risk (allergic reactions, TRALI, iron overload) + does not treat cause.
- **Do not skip endoscopy in any adult > 50 with unexplained IDA** even if FIT negative – small bowel lesion (angiodysplasia, tumour, Crohn’s) may be missed.
- **Do not stop iron once Hb normalises** – iron stores take 3–6 months to replenish; early cessation leads to recurrence.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening dyspnoea, palpitations, black/tarry stools, haematemesis, new abdominal pain
- **Follow-up:** GP repeat FBC + ferritin in 6–8 weeks; if Hb not improved by > 10 g/L, arrange prompt clinic review

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / on‑call medical registrar if:**
  - Hb < 60 g/L with symptoms (chest pain, dyspnoea, syncope)
  - Signs of active GI bleeding (haematemesis, melaena) – need OGD within 24 h
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially tachycardia + hypotension)
- **Criteria for ICU:**
  - Haemodynamic instability despite 2 units PRBC + fluid
  - Suspected acute GI bleed with persistent shock
